records.ety: [OE. warpen; fr. Icel. varpa to throw, cast, varp a casting, fr. verpa to throw; akin to Dan. varpe to warp a ship, Sw. varpa, AS. weorpan to cast, OS. werpan, OFries. werpa, D. & LG. werpen, G. werfen, Goth. warpan; cf. Skr. vj to twist. √144. Cf. Wrap.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Warped (wrpt); p. pr. & vb. n. Warping.]

defs: To throw; hence, to send forth, or throw out, as words; to utter.
To turn or twist out of shape; esp., to twist or bend out of a flat plane by contraction or otherwise.
quotes: Walter warped his mouth at this To something so mock solemn, that I laughed.

defs: To turn aside from the true direction; to cause to bend or incline; to pervert.
quotes: This first avowed, nor folly warped my mind.
I have no private considerations to warp me in this controversy.
We are divested of all those passions which cloud the intellects, and warp the understandings, of men.

defs: To weave; to fabricate.
To tow or move, as a vessel, with a line, or warp, attached to a buoy, anchor, or other fixed object.
To cast prematurely, as young; -- said of cattle, sheep, etc.
To let the tide or other water in upon (lowlying land), for the purpose of fertilization, by a deposit of warp, or slimy substance.
To run off the reel into hauls to be tarred, as yarns.
To arrange (yarns) on a warp beam.
To twist the end surfaces of (an aerocurve in an airfoil) in order to restore or maintain equilibrium.
To turn, twist, or be twisted out of shape; esp., to be twisted or bent out of a flat plane; as, a board warps in seasoning or shrinking.
quotes: One of you will prove a shrunk panel, and, like green timber, warp, warp.
They clamp one piece of wood to the end of another, to keep it from casting, or warping.

defs: to turn or incline from a straight, true, or proper course; to deviate; to swerve.
quotes: There is our commission, From which we would not have you warp.

defs: To fly with a bending or waving motion; to turn and wave, like a flock of birds or insects.
quotes: A pitchy cloud Of locusts, warping on the eastern wind.

defs: To cast the young prematurely; to slink; -- said of cattle, sheep, etc.
To wind yarn off bobbins for forming the warp of a web; to wind a warp on a warp beam.

records.ety: [AS. wearp; akin to Icel. varp a casting, throwing, Sw. varp the draught of a net, Dan. varp a towline, OHG. warf warp, G. werft. See Warp, v.]

defs: The threads which are extended lengthwise in the loom, and crossed by the woof.
A rope used in hauling or moving a vessel, usually with one end attached to an anchor, a post, or other fixed object; a towing line; a warping hawser.
A slimy substance deposited on land by tides, etc., by which a rich alluvial soil is formed.
A premature casting of young; -- said of cattle, sheep, etc.
Four; esp., four herrings; a cast. See Cast, n., 17.
The state of being warped or twisted; as, the warp of a board.
